ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated entry for the Singaporean multi-layered agar-agar dessert; the closest defensible match is the general agar-agar jelly food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singaporean layered agar-agar; A00LC (agar-based jelly) is the closest defensible anchor in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for agar-agar dessert; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of agar-agar is one slice or piece weighing approximately 100 g, as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and dessert stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Agar-Agar — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Agar-Agar (layered multi-coloured jelly). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean multi-layered agar-agar dessert in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: sugar-sweetened jelly dessert with agar gelling agent. The dominant glycaemic driver is sucrose (15.7 g/100 g); agar itself contributes negligible digestible carbohydrate. GL per 100 g serving = GI × available carbohydrate / 100 = 65 × 15.7 / 100 ≈ 10.2. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (15.7 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (0.5 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is low at 7 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit agar-agar desserts prepared with high added sugar, as the 15.7 g/100 g sugar load with only 0.5 g/100 g fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation despite the negligible direct sodium content of 7 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 15.7 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 0.5 g/100 g produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike; estimated GI of 65 (medium) with GL of 10.2 per 100 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit standard agar-agar desserts due to the high sugar content of 15.7 g/100 g and minimal fibre of 0.5 g/100 g; home preparation using sugar substitutes such as stevia or erythritol and increasing agar concentration can substantially reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (15.7 g/100 g) with negligible saturated fat and very low sodium; chronic high-sugar intake raises triglycerides and promotes endothelial inflammation relevant to CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, agar-agar desserts are low in saturated fat and sodium but the high sugar content of 15.7 g/100 g poses a risk for elevated triglycerides and hepatic de novo lipogenesis; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled, or reformulated with reduced sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar (15.7 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid high-sugar agar-agar desserts as the 15.7 g/100 g refined sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; preparation with fresh fruit, ginger, or lemon juice and minimal added sugar is recommended to reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Agar-Agar is a popular Singaporean dessert consisting of layers of multi-coloured jelly made from agar-agar powder, water, sugar, and food colouring. Agar-agar powder is derived from red algae (Gelidium and Gracilaria species) and acts as a plant-based gelling agent, making this dessert naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The jelly is prepared by dissolving agar-agar powder and sugar in boiling water, adding food colouring to create distinct coloured layers, and allowing each layer to set before adding the next. The result is a firm, slightly chewy, translucent jelly with a mild sweetness and a clean, refreshing taste. It is commonly served chilled at hawker centres, dessert stalls, and festive occasions across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Southeast Asian region. Nutritionally, agar-agar dessert provides 77 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient at 18.6 g/100 g, of which 15.7 g is sugar. Protein and fat are both minimal at 0.2 g/100 g each. Dietary fibre is low at 0.5 g/100 g, and sodium is negligible at 7 mg/100 g. The high sugar content relative to fibre gives this dessert a medium estimated glycaemic index of approximately 65 and a glycaemic load of approximately 10.2 per 100 g serving. Clinically, the high added sugar content makes this dessert unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant reformulation to reduce sugar. Agar-agar powder itself is a prebiotic fibre with potential gut health benefits, but these are largely negated by the high sugar content in the prepared dessert form.
common_user_queries: Can I eat agar-agar if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between agar-agar and gelatin?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to the named food item. GI is an analogous estimate and is the only component at reduced certainty.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo; the fat content of 0.2 g/100 g is predominantly unsaturated in agar-based desserts with no added dairy or animal fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; this is a fully plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Singaporean agar-agar dessert preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened jelly desserts from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean layered agar-agar was found. GL per 100 g serving = GI × available carbohydrate / 100 = 65 × 15.7 / 100 ≈ 10.2. 4. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for Singaporean multi-layered agar-agar dessert. 5. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine: Chinese (燕菜), Malay (Agar-Agar), Tamil (அகர்-அகர்), Hindi (अगर-अगर).
